---
name: convert-dates
model: claude-sonnet-4-6
description: Use when a genealogist asks to convert a date "to the
  Gregorian calendar," asks what a Quaker numbered-month date means in
  modern terms, wonders if an unusual historical date is valid under the
  period's calendar system, or wants to know if same-date records from
  different countries actually describe the same day. Handles
  Julian-to-Gregorian arithmetic, Old Style/New Style year-start
  corrections, Quaker numbered months, and double-dated years (e.g.
  "1749/50"). Country transition cutoffs — Catholic Europe 1582, Germany
  1700, England/colonies 1752, Sweden 1753, Russia 1918. Skip for cosmetic
  reformatting without conversion (use no skill), date schema validation
  (use validate-schema), source conflicts where both records used the
  same calendar (use conflict-resolution), and explanations of why a
  calendar convention existed (use historical-context).
allowed-tools:
  - convert_calendar
---

# Convert Dates

**Narration:** Read `researcher_profile.narration_guidance` from `research.json` and apply it as your narration style for this invocation. If absent, default to a one-line preamble per action.

Converts historical dates between calendar systems. Before the
Gregorian calendar was universally adopted, different jurisdictions
used different systems — and the transition dates vary by country.
Getting this wrong can place an event in the wrong YEAR, not just
the wrong day.

## Routing

**Use convert-dates when** the date difference between sources matches
the expected calendar offset for the jurisdictions involved — that is
a conversion, not a conflict.

**Hand off to conflict-resolution when** the difference does NOT match
any expected calendar offset, or both records come from the same
jurisdiction at the same time (same calendar system). Before flagging
a date disagreement, check whether the records come from jurisdictions
on different calendars. If the difference is exactly 10–13 days, or
exactly 1 year (for Jan–Mar dates), or both — it is almost certainly
a calendar-system difference, not a true conflict.

**No skill needed when** both dates are already in the same calendar
system (e.g. both post-transition Gregorian) and the gap does not match
any calendar offset — the user can resolve this with general GPS
reasoning about source proximity and evidence weighing.

**Hand off to historical-context when** the user asks WHY a calendar
or dating convention existed. "Convert this Quaker date" is
convert-dates. "Why did Quakers use numbered months?" is
historical-context.

## Calling `convert_calendar`

The tool does only arithmetic — your job is the judgment: identify the
regime (jurisdiction, era, calendar in force) from the tables below,
decide which corrections the user actually asked for, and request
exactly those.

```
convert_calendar({
  date: { year, month?, day?, doubleYear? },
  corrections: {
    doubleDatedYear?: true,                  // resolve "1750/1" → later year
    osNsYear?: true,                         // Jan 1–Mar 24 → year + 1
    quakerMonth?: { era: "pre_1752" | "post_1752" }, // month is the Quaker ordinal
    julianToGregorianDay?: true,             // add the era day offset (10–13)
  },
})
```

Returns `{ ok: true, original, converted, applied, notes }`. Narrate
from `applied[].rule` and `notes[]`, present `converted` next to
`original`.

If it returns `{ ok: false, errors }`, surface the error and the
missing input to the user — fix the input or the regime choice and
call again. Do not fall back to hand arithmetic.

**Present the result** in step-by-step form: original date and system
(`original`), the rule applied (`applied[].rule`, plus
`applied[].offsetDays` on a day shift), and the converted date
(`converted`). Example: "14 September 1582 (Julian) +10 days =
24 September 1582 (Gregorian)". Always preserve both the original
and converted forms.

## Calendar regime tables

Use these tables to identify which calendar was in force, then pass the
matching `corrections` key.

### Julian vs. Gregorian → `julianToGregorianDay: true`

| Jurisdiction | Gregorian adoption | Offset | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catholic Europe (Spain, Portugal, Italy, Poland) | Oct 1582 | 10 days | Oct 4 → Oct 15 |
| France | Dec 1582 | 10 days | |
| Catholic German states | 1583–1585 | 10 days | Varied by state |
| Protestant German states | 1700 | 10→11 days | Feb 18 → Mar 1 |
| Denmark/Norway | 1700 | 10→11 days | |
| Great Britain & colonies | Sep 1752 | 11 days | Sep 2 → Sep 14 |
| Sweden | 1753 | 11 days (post-1753); 1700–1753 used a unique "Swedish calendar" 1 day ahead of Julian / 10 days behind Gregorian | Failed gradual transition; Feb 30, 1712 is a real Swedish date (added to revert to Julian). Swedish 30 Feb 1712 = Julian 29 Feb 1712 = Gregorian 11 Mar 1712 |
| Scotland | 1752 (day correction) | 10→11 days | Year-start changed to Jan 1 in 1600 |
| Russia | Feb 1918 | 13 days | Jan 31 → Feb 14 |
| Greece | 1923 | 13 days | |

Offset grows by 1 day at each Julian leap year the Gregorian calendar
skipped: before 1700 → 10 days; 1700–1799 → 11; 1800–1899 → 12;
1900+ → 13.

### Old Style / New Style year → `osNsYear: true`

Before 1752, England's legal year began March 25. Dates January 1 –
March 24 are in the "previous" year by modern reckoning:
"15 February 1720" OS = "15 February 1721" NS.

### Double-dated years → `doubleDatedYear: true`

Records often show both years: "25 March 1750/1" means 1750 OS but
1751 NS. Pass `date: { year: 1750, doubleYear: 1 }` — the tool
returns the later (New Style) year.

### Quaker numbered months → `quakerMonth: { era }`

| Quaker | Before 1752 | After 1752 |
|---|---|---|
| 1st month | March | January |
| 2nd month | April | February |
| … | … | … |
| 10th month | December | October |
| 11th month | January (next year) | November |
| 12th month | February (next year) | December |

"1st month" shifts meaning at 1752 — always check the era.

## Rules

- **Answer only the calendar question asked.** Each correction
  (OS/NS year, Julian→Gregorian day, Quaker month, double-date) is a
  separate operation. Do not bundle corrections the user didn't
  request — that is over-conversion.
- **Show original next to converted.** The original is what the record
  says; the conversion is interpretation. Keep them distinct.
- **When in doubt, don't convert.** If the jurisdiction or calendar
  convention is unclear, flag the ambiguity rather than guessing.
- **Jurisdiction matters.** Never convert without knowing where the
  record was created.

## Re-invocation behavior

Output-only. Writes nothing. Idempotent — same date and jurisdiction produce the same result.

# Calendar-Based Date Conflicts: Identification and Resolution

## Principle

Under the Genealogical Proof Standard, conflicting evidence must be
acknowledged, analyzed, and resolved. However, not every apparent
date disagreement is a true conflict. Many date discrepancies arise
from records being created under different calendar systems. The
researcher's first task when encountering conflicting dates is to
determine whether the difference reflects a genuine informational
disagreement or simply a calendar-system difference.

If the discrepancy is explained by calendar conversion, the dates
actually corroborate each other rather than conflict. This should be
documented clearly so that future researchers do not waste effort
re-investigating the same apparent contradiction.

---

## The Two Components of "Old Style vs New Style"

### Component 1: The Year-Start Change

Before adoption of the Gregorian calendar, many jurisdictions
(notably England and its colonies) began the civil year on March 25
rather than January 1. This means:

- A date of "15 February 1720" in an English parish register refers
  to what we would call 15 February 1721 in modern reckoning.
- Any date between January 1 and March 24 in a pre-adoption record
  needs one year added to match modern conventions.
- This is ONLY a year issue. The day and month remain the same.

### Component 2: The 11-Day Shift (Julian to Gregorian)

The Julian calendar accumulated an error of approximately one day
every 128 years relative to the solar year. By the time of the
major European transitions:

- In 1582 (Catholic Europe): the gap was 10 days
- In 1700 (Protestant Germany, Denmark/Norway): the gap was 10 days
- In 1752 (Britain and colonies): the gap was 11 days
- In 1918 (Russia): the gap was 13 days
- In 1923 (Greece): the gap was 13 days

When a jurisdiction adopted the Gregorian calendar, it skipped
forward by the appropriate number of days. This affects the DAY
within the month (and sometimes the month itself if the date falls
near a month boundary).

### Why the Distinction Matters

These two corrections are independent:

- The year-start change affects ONLY dates between January 1 and
  March 24. It shifts the year by exactly one.
- The day correction affects ALL dates. It shifts the day by a
  fixed number (depending on century).

In England (1752), both changes were applied simultaneously. But in
other jurisdictions they may have occurred at different times. Some
countries changed the year-start to January 1 well before adopting
the Gregorian day correction (e.g., Scotland changed the year-start
in 1600 but kept the Julian calendar until 1752).

---

## Decision Process: Is This a Real Conflict?

When two sources show different dates for what appears to be the
same event, follow this sequence:

### Step 1: Identify the jurisdiction and date of each record

Determine where each record was created and what calendar system
was in force there at that time. Use the transition table in the
main SKILL.md.

### Step 2: Check if the jurisdictions used different calendars

If Record A is from a Julian-calendar jurisdiction and Record B is
from a Gregorian-calendar jurisdiction (at the time of the event),
a day-offset difference is EXPECTED, not contradictory.

### Step 3: Calculate the expected offset

- Same century, different calendars: apply the day correction for
  that century (10-13 days depending on era)
- One record is pre-March-25 in an OS jurisdiction: apply the
  year-start correction (+1 year)
- Both corrections may apply simultaneously

### Step 4: Compare the actual difference to the expected offset

- If the actual difference matches the expected calendar offset:
  the dates AGREE. Document the conversion and note that the
  apparent conflict is resolved by calendar-system differences.
- If the actual difference does NOT match: a genuine conflict may
  exist. Proceed to full conflict resolution analysis.

### Step 5: Document the resolution

In the assertion's `informant_bias_notes`, record:
- The original date as stated in each source
- The calendar system each source used
- The converted equivalent showing agreement
- A brief statement that the apparent conflict is resolved by
  calendar conversion

---

## Patterns That Signal Calendar Differences (Not True Conflicts)

| Observed Difference | Likely Explanation |
|--------------------|--------------------|
| Exactly 10 days (pre-1700 records) | Julian/Gregorian offset for that era |
| Exactly 11 days (1700-1799 records) | Julian/Gregorian offset for that era |
| Exactly 13 days (1800-1923 records) | Julian/Gregorian offset for that era |
| Exactly 1 year, date is Jan 1 - Mar 24 | Old Style/New Style year-start |
| 11 days + 1 year, date is Jan-Mar | Both corrections combined (common in English records compared to Continental) |
| Day matches but year is off by 1 | Year-start issue only (no day correction needed if comparing within same calendar tradition) |

---

## Common Research Scenarios

### English parish register vs French church record (pre-1752)

England used Julian (OS) while France used Gregorian from 1582.
Differences of 11 days and/or 1 year (for Jan-Mar dates) are
expected and do not indicate conflicting information.

### American colonial records vs Spanish colonial records

English colonies followed England's calendar until 1752. Spanish
colonies used Gregorian from 1582. Same analysis applies as
English vs French.

### Russian Empire records vs Western European records (pre-1918)

Russia kept the Julian calendar until February 1918. Any Russian
record compared to a Western source will show a 12-day offset
(19th century) or 13-day offset (20th century). This is not a
data-entry error or informant confusion.

### Scottish records (1600-1752)

Scotland changed its year-start to January 1 in 1600 but kept the
Julian day reckoning until 1752. So a Scottish record from 1700
starts the year on January 1 (unlike England) but is still 11 days
behind the Gregorian calendar. This creates a hybrid situation
where comparing Scottish to English records requires only the
year-start correction (for Jan-Mar dates), while comparing Scottish
to Continental records requires only the day correction.

---

## When Calendar Conversion Does NOT Explain the Difference

Calendar conversion cannot explain:
- Differences of months (unless near a month boundary with the day
  shift pushing across)
- Differences of multiple years
- Differences in the day that do not match the expected offset for
  the era
- Differences between two records from the SAME jurisdiction at the
  SAME time (both should use the same calendar)

In these cases, the conflict is genuine and requires full analysis:
weighing source reliability, informant proximity, and record
purpose to determine which version is more likely correct and why
the other version exists.

# Convert Dates Rubric

Grading dimensions for convert-dates unit tests. Evaluated by the LLM judge alongside the base rubric (correctness, completeness).

## Conversion accuracy

Did the skill apply the correct calendar conversion rules for the time period and jurisdiction? Julian-to-Gregorian shifts vary by country and year of adoption.

- **pass:** Conversion uses the right offset for the jurisdiction-and-year combination (11 days for England 1752; 10 days for Catholic Europe 1582).
- **partial:** Conversion is in the right direction with the right approximate offset but off by 1 day, or applies a generic offset instead of the jurisdiction-specific one.
- **fail:** Conversion is wrong direction, wrong offset, or unapplied when the date clearly requires it.

## Ambiguity handling

Did the skill correctly handle dates that are genuinely ambiguous (e.g., dates near a calendar transition, dual-dating periods, or jurisdictions where the recorder's convention is unknown)?

This dimension applies ONLY when the input contains real ambiguity in the source date — meaning multiple valid interpretations actually exist. If the input is deterministic and a single conversion is correct, mark this dimension N/A. Do NOT credit Claude's explanatory commentary about hypothetical ambiguity in other dates, or educational context about historical transitions, as "ambiguity handling" when no ambiguity exists in the input.

- **pass:** Input is genuinely ambiguous, AND the skill explicitly flags both possible interpretations and records both so the genealogist can pick.
- **partial:** Input is genuinely ambiguous, AND the ambiguity is noted but the skill picks an interpretation without spelling out the alternative.
- **fail:** Input is genuinely ambiguous, AND the skill silently converts to one interpretation with no mention of the alternative.
- **N/A:** Input contains no genuine ambiguity.

## Genealogical presentation

Did the skill present the converted date in a format suitable for genealogical records?

Score this dimension strictly on FORMAT and PRESERVATION: does the output preserve the original date as recorded in the source AND show the converted form in standard genealogical notation? Do NOT credit explanatory commentary, methodological guidance, or contextual teaching content as part of presentation quality. Those are valuable but belong outside this dimension.

- **pass:** Output records both the original (as stated in the source) and the converted form in a notation a genealogist would recognize ("11 Mar 1752/3" for dual dating). Format is clean and consistent.
- **partial:** Both forms are recorded but the notation is non-standard, omits the dual-dating convention when relevant, or buries the original in surrounding prose rather than presenting it clearly.
- **fail:** Only the converted form is preserved; the original is lost, OR the format is unusable as a genealogical record (e.g., dates only wrapped in prose with no clean date string).

The `convert_calendar` arithmetic tool is not available in this environment, but the regime tables in the skill give a definitive, unambiguous answer. Here is the step-by-step conversion:

---

## Date Conversion: 14 September 1582 (Madrid)

### Step 1 — Identify the calendar in force

| Factor | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Jurisdiction** | Madrid, Spain — Catholic Europe |
| **Gregorian adoption** | October 1582 (Oct 4 Julian → Oct 15 Gregorian) |
| **Record date** | 14 September 1582 — **before** the Oct 4 Julian cutoff |
| **Calendar in force** | **Julian** (Spain had not yet switched) |

> Spain adopted the Gregorian calendar on **4 October 1582 (Julian)**, after which the very next day became **15 October 1582 (Gregorian)**. A parish record dated 14 September 1582 falls six weeks *before* that switch — so it is a Julian date.

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Julian→Gregorian day offset

The pre-1700 offset is **+10 days** (the Gregorian calendar skipped 10 days to correct accumulated drift).

| | Date |
|---|---|
| **Original (Julian)** | 14 September 1582 |
| **Rule applied** | `julianToGregorianDay` — pre-1700 offset: +10 days |
| **Converted (Gregorian)** | **24 September 1582** |

---

### Result

> **14 September 1582 (Julian, Madrid) = 24 September 1582 (Gregorian)**

No year correction is needed — this is a September date, well clear of the January–March window where Old Style / New Style year-start differences could apply. The conversion is a single 10-day day-shift only.

output.text_response:
The `convert_calendar` MCP tool is not available in this environment, so I'll apply the regime tables from the skill directly.

---

## Are These the Same Day? **No.**

In 1730, France and England were on **different calendar systems**, so the same written date refers to two different days.

---

### Step-by-step

| | French baptism | English marriage |
|---|---|---|
| **Written date** | 14 May 1730 | 14 May 1730 |
| **Calendar in force** | Gregorian (France adopted Dec 1582) | Julian / Old Style (England adopted Sep 1752) |

**Correction needed:** Convert the English Julian date to its Gregorian equivalent.

> Regime: 1700–1799 → offset is **+11 days**
> (The Gregorian calendar skipped the Julian leap year of 1700, adding 1 day to the prior 10-day offset.)

| Step | Calculation |
|---|---|
| English record (Julian OS) | 14 May 1730 |
| + Julian → Gregorian day offset | + 11 days |
| **= Gregorian equivalent** | **25 May 1730** |

*(May dates are unaffected by the OS/NS year-start issue, which only applies to 1 Jan – 24 Mar.)*

---

### Conclusion

| Record | Gregorian date |
|---|---|
| French baptism | **14 May 1730** |
| English marriage | **25 May 1730** |

These events are **11 days apart** on the universal Gregorian calendar. If you're building a timeline or correlating these two records for the same person, treat them as distinct dates — the written "14 May 1730" is a coincidence of notation, not of occurrence.

The `convert_calendar` MCP tool is not available in this environment, so I'll work through the conversion step-by-step using the established rules for English records.

---

## Converting 15 February 1749/50 (English) to Modern Gregorian

**Original record:** 15 February 1749/50 — an English Old Style (Julian) date with a double-dated year.

Two separate corrections are required for a full conversion to the modern Gregorian calendar:

---

### Correction 1 — Resolve the double-dated year (`doubleDatedYear`)

Before September 1752, England's legal year began on **25 March** (Lady Day), not 1 January. Any date falling between **1 January and 24 March** could belong to either the old or the new year depending on convention, so careful clerks wrote both:

| Notation | Old Style (legal) year | New Style (modern) year |
|---|---|---|
| **1749/50** | 1749 | **1750** |

Since 15 February falls squarely in the 1 Jan–24 Mar window, the double-date is correct and expected. The modern year is **1750**.

> **After Correction 1:** 15 February **1750** (year resolved, still Julian)

---

### Correction 2 — Julian to Gregorian day offset (`julianToGregorianDay`)

England did not adopt the Gregorian calendar until **2/14 September 1752**. A record dated February 1750 is therefore still a **Julian** date. The day offset for the 1700–1799 period is **+11 days**:

> 15 February 1750 (Julian) **+ 11 days** = **26 February 1750** (Gregorian)

| | Date |

| **Original (record says)** | 15 February 1749/50 (Julian, Old Style) |
| **Modern Gregorian** | **26 February 1750** |

2. Julian-to-Gregorian day shift (1700–1799 offset): **+11 days** → 15 Feb → 26 Feb

There's no project file open here, so I can't write a `c_` conflict entry — but I can apply the full analytical framework to your question directly. Let me work through independence, weighing, and the four-part rationale.

---

## Conflict Analysis: Death Date — 3 June 1865 vs. 8 June 1865

### Step 1 — State the problem

Two English records from the same county disagree on the date of death by exactly **five days**. The question is which date reflects the actual death, or whether both dates are simultaneously correct for different events.

---

### Step 2 — The critical question before weighing: What *kind* of record is each?

Before weighing evidence, you need to establish what each record is actually *recording*, because a 5-day gap in 1865 England has a well-documented, mundane explanation:

| Record type | What date it records |
|---|---|
| **Civil death registration** (GRO) | **Date of death** as reported by the informant |
| **Anglican burial register** | **Date of burial**, not death |
| **Nonconformist burial register** | **Date of burial**, not death |
| **Gravestone inscription** | Could be either — often burial date |
| **Newspaper notice** | Could be either |

**In Victorian England, burial typically followed death by 3–7 days.** A 5-day gap between "3 June" and "8 June" falls squarely within that normal window. This is one of the most common apparent date conflicts in 19th-century English research, and it is frequently *not a conflict at all* — both dates are correct, for different events.

---

### Step 3 — Independence analysis

If one record is a civil death certificate and the other a burial register entry, they are **independent records with independent informants recording independent events** (death vs. interment). They share no common underlying information source, so grouping them as a single unit of information would be wrong. They are genuinely independent — but they are also not necessarily contradicting each other.

If both records are of the *same type* (e.g., two civil death certificates, or two burial registers), then independence must be assessed differently: do they derive from the same registration event or from independent informants?

---

### Step 4 — Weighing (conditional on record type)

**If one is a civil death registration and the other a burial register:**
Neither date is "wrong." Civil registration records the death; the burial register records the burial. Accept 3 June as the death date and 8 June as the burial date (or vice versa — confirm by record type). **This is a pseudo-conflict, not a true conflict.**

**If both are civil death registrations:**
- The GRO death certificate carries the strongest weight: the informant was required to appear in person before the registrar and swear to the information, and the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act 1836 required registration within 8 days of death (effectively 5 days in many districts by practice).
- The informant on a civil death certificate was typically a relative present at the death, or the occupier of the premises — someone with direct, first-hand knowledge.
- A 5-day discrepancy between two certificates for the same person would be unusual and likely indicates one is a *re-registration*, an amendment, or a confusion with a different record for a different individual.

**If one is a civil death certificate and the other is a gravestone or newspaper notice:**
The civil death certificate is the stronger record: it was created at or very near the time of death, by a named informant under legal obligation, and recorded by a civil registrar. Gravestones and newspaper notices are secondary and subject to family recollection or typographical error.

---

### Step 5 — The most likely resolution

> **Probable answer: 3 June 1865 is the death date; 8 June 1865 is the burial date.**

This assumes the record showing 3 June is the civil registration (or another death-date record) and the record showing 8 June is the burial register. A 5-day burial delay was entirely normal in mid-Victorian England, particularly in summer months when decomposition accelerated arrangements, or when family needed time to travel.

**Why the apparent conflict exists:** This falls squarely under the *record-type confusion* pattern — researchers comparing dates without accounting for the fact that different record classes record different moments in the dying-and-burial sequence. It is not an informant error; it is an apples-and-oranges comparison.

---

### What to do next

1. **Identify both record types** — Pull the record headers/titles. Is one a GRO death certificate (or index entry) and the other a parish/cemetery burial register? If yes, the conflict dissolves: accept both dates for their respective events.

2. **If both are the same record type**, check the informants. Who signed or provided the information on each? The one supplied by a person present at the death (spouse, child in the household, attending physician) outweighs one supplied later by a more distant informant.

3. **Check the GRO index** — The FreeBMD / GRO index for the June 1865 quarter will show the registered date. That is your anchor for the civil death date.

Would you like help identifying which FamilySearch or GRO records to search to confirm the record types, or shall I help build a research question targeting the burial register for this county?

---

## Why Quakers Used Numbered Months

### The Short Answer

Quakers rejected the traditional names of months and days of the week because most were derived from pagan gods or deified Roman emperors. Using those names was, in their view, an act of idolatry — a compromise with the world that contradicted plain Christian living.

---

### The Religious Roots: Plain Speech and Testimony of Simplicity

The Society of Friends was founded in England in the 1650s, chiefly under George Fox. From the beginning, Friends held a doctrine of *plainness* — a refusal to participate in worldly conventions that elevated human pride or perpetuated false religion. This was not merely stylistic; it was a theological testimony.

Numbered months fit within a cluster of similar practices *(from general knowledge — not confirmed by a wiki tool call this session)*:

| Quaker practice | The worldly convention rejected |
|---|---|
| "Thee/thou" to everyone | The honorific "you" (plural used for social superiors) |
| No hat-doffing before magistrates | Social deference to rank |
| No oath-swearing; affirming instead | The legal custom of swearing on the Bible |
| No titles ("Mr.", "Lord", "Your Honor") | Social hierarchy |
| Plain dress | Display of wealth and status |
| **Numbered months and days** | **Pagan/imperial deity names embedded in the calendar** |

---

### The Specific Problem with Month and Day Names

The English calendar of the 17th century still carried the names of Roman gods and deified emperors throughout the week and year *(general knowledge)*:

**Days of the week:**
- Sunday → the Sun (a celestial body worshipped in antiquity)
- Monday → the Moon
- Tuesday → Tiw (Norse god of war)
- Wednesday → Woden (Odin)
- Thursday → Thor
- Friday → Frigg (or Freya)
- Saturday → Saturn (Roman god)

**Months:**
- January → Janus (Roman god of beginnings)
- February → Februa (Roman purification festival)
- March → Mars (Roman god of war)
- May → Maia (Roman goddess)
- June → Juno (Roman goddess)
- July → Julius Caesar (deified emperor)
- August → Augustus Caesar (deified emperor)

For Friends, casually using these names in everyday speech — and especially in official Meeting records — was participation in a false religious system. Numbering replaced all of it cleanly: no gods, no emperors, no compromise.

---

### The Calendar Complication: Before and After 1752

Here is where this history becomes directly important for reading Quaker genealogical records.

**Before 1752**, England used the Julian calendar, under which the legal new year began on **March 25 (Lady Day)**. March was therefore the *first month* of the year. Quaker numbered months followed this civil calendar:

| Quaker designation | Gregorian month |
|---|---|
| 1st Month | **March** |
| 2nd Month | April |
| 3rd Month | May |
| 4th Month | June |
| 5th Month | July |
| 6th Month | August |
| 7th Month | September |
| 8th Month | October |
| 9th Month | November |
| 10th Month | December |
| 11th Month | **January** |
| 12th Month | February |

**After 1752**, when England adopted the Gregorian calendar and January 1 became New Year's Day, Quakers adjusted their numbering accordingly:

| Quaker designation | Gregorian month |
|---|---|
| 1st Month | **January** |
| 2nd Month | February |
| ... | ... |
| 12th Month | December |

*(This is confirmed by the boundary-and-calendar-changes reference file loaded this session.)*

---

### The Most Common Genealogical Error

A record reading **"8th Month 14, 1748"** is frequently misread as August 14, 1748.

It actually means **October 14, 1748** — because in 1748, before the 1752 calendar change, the 8th Month was October (March being the 1st).

This is the single most important thing to know when working with Quaker records. The error shifts an event by two full months, which can cascade into misidentifying individuals, miscalculating ages, or missing associated records filed under the correct date.

---

### Why This Matters for Your Research

1. **Every Quaker date before 1752 must be verified against the pre-1752 numbering scheme.** The numbered month is *not* the modern calendar equivalent.

2. **Quaker Meeting records are unusually rich and systematic.** Because Friends rejected state church institutions, they kept their own meticulous registers of births, marriages, disownments, removals, and deaths — often predating civil registration by decades. These records survive in significant quantity, held by Friends Historical Library (Swarthmore College), Haverford College, and Quaker collections at state archives.

3. **Disownment records are genealogically significant.** Friends who married outside the Meeting, joined the military, or deviated from testimony were formally disowned — and these proceedings are recorded. A disownment record can confirm a person's identity, family connections, and the date they left the Quaker community.

4. **If you need to convert a specific Quaker date**, the **convert-dates** skill handles Julian/Gregorian arithmetic and Quaker numbered-month conversion precisely. This skill explains the *why* behind the convention; convert-dates handles the *how* of doing the arithmetic correctly.

The validate-schema skill runs the MCP tool against your project files — it doesn't answer hypothetical format questions directly. Let me look at the schema spec to give you a precise answer.Now I have everything I need to answer this definitively. Here's what I found:

---

## Short answer: **Yes, the validator will accept it.**

The `validate_research_schema` tool does **not** check date string formats. Here is the complete list of what it actually validates in `research.json`, per the skill spec:

| What it checks | Examples |
|---|---|
| 11 required top-level sections exist | `questions`, `log`, `sources`, … |
| Required fields on every object | `id`, `status`, `created`, … |
| ID prefix conventions | `src_`, `a_`, `log_`, … |
| Enum values | `status`, `experience_level`, … |
| Cross-references resolve | every `source_id` points to a real source |
| `external_site` object present when tool = `external_site` | structural rule |

Date **string format** is not in that list. The validator treats date fields as opaque strings — it checks presence (not null) but not content. A value of `"15 Feb 1749/50"` will pass without complaint.

---

## What `15 Feb 1749/50` actually means

It is a **double-dated year** — a historically standard notation used in English and colonial records between January 1 and March 24 during the Old Style period (before Britain's 1752 calendar reform). The two years represent:

- **1749** — the Old Style civil year (which began March 25)
- **1750** — the New Style year (January 1 start, modern convention)

So `15 Feb 1749/50` = 15 February 1749 OS = **15 February 1750 NS (modern reckoning)**. It is not ambiguous or malformed — it is the correct historical notation for a date that fell in the "gap" period.

---

## Practical guidance

| Concern | Answer |
|---|---|
| Will the validator reject it? | No |
| Is it historically correct notation? | Yes, for an English/colonial record Jan 1–Mar 24 before 1752 |
| Should you store it as-is or convert? | Store the original (`15 Feb 1749/50`) to preserve fidelity to the source; add a note with the NS equivalent (`15 Feb 1750`) for clarity |
| Need to convert it? | Use the **convert-dates** skill with `doubleDatedYear: true` |

## What "25 March 1750/1" means

### Step 1 — Identify the notation: Double-dated year

The slash notation **1750/1** is a *double-dated year*. It records **two year numbers simultaneously**:

| Part | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **1750** | Old Style (OS) year — England's legal year, which began on **25 March** |
| **1751** | New Style (NS) year — the modern year, which begins on **1 January** |

### Step 2 — Why March 25 is special

Before the Calendar (New Style) Act of 1752, England's legal new year began on **25 March (Lady Day)**. That makes this marriage record date the very **first day of the Old Style year 1751** — the moment the year turned. The clerk wrote both years precisely *because* this was the transition day itself, making the year ambiguous to contemporary readers as well.

| System | Date |
|---|---|
| Old Style (as written) | 25 March 1750 |
| **New Style / modern** | **25 March 1751** |

There is no day-of-month shift here — the Julian-to-Gregorian **day** correction (11 days for England) is a separate operation not implied by the double-date notation alone, and should only be applied if you are specifically comparing this event to a Gregorian-calendar source.

---

## Which year to record in your research notes

> **Use 1751.**

Best practice is to write the date in one of these two ways:

- **25 March 1751** — the modernised New Style year, unambiguous for indexing and sorting

## February 30, 1712 — Real Date, Not an OCR Error

**Short answer: trust the record.** February 30, 1712 is one of the most unusual — but genuine — dates in European calendar history. It existed *only* in Sweden, for exactly the reason you'd expect from a calendar system that briefly went off the rails.

---

### What happened to the Swedish calendar (1700–1753)

Sweden planned a slow, 40-year glide from the Julian to the Gregorian calendar by simply *omitting* leap days until the calendars aligned. In 1700 they dropped the leap day as planned — making Sweden **1 day ahead of Julian and 10 days behind Gregorian**.

Then the plan collapsed. Sweden restored leap days in **1704 and 1708**, drifting back toward Julian. By 1712 the Swedish calendar was a one-of-a-kind hybrid that matched no other country.

King Charles XII's government decided to cut their losses and **revert fully to the Julian calendar**. To do that they needed to *add back* the day they had dropped in 1700. Their solution: give February 1712 **two** leap days — both February 29 **and February 30**. That single extraordinary month wiped out the discrepancy and put Sweden back on Julian.

Sweden finally moved to the Gregorian calendar properly in **1753**.

---

### Conversion table for this date

| Record says | Calendar system | Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **30 February 1712** | Swedish | ← the source date |
| 29 February 1712 | Julian (OS) | Swedish 30 Feb = Julian 29 Feb |
| **11 March 1712** | Gregorian (NS) | +11 days from Julian |

> **Rule applied:** Swedish calendar reversion correction (the extra leap day added to re-sync with Julian), then Julian → Gregorian +11 days (1700–1799 offset).

---

### Practical guidance

- **Do not "correct" this date** in your notes — it is the record's authentic date, and it is valid.
- If you need to cross-reference it against records from other countries (church correspondence, ship manifests, etc.), use **11 March 1712 (Gregorian)** as the equivalent.
- Annotate your source citation with: *"Date recorded in the Swedish calendar; equivalent to 29 February 1712 Julian / 11 March 1712 Gregorian."*
